title: Sunflower
prose: Sunflower ( Helianthus annuus L .) is an annual summer crop produced mainly for the high oil content of the seed. It is the most important oilseed crop in South Africa. Depending on the cultivar and planting time, the growing season will vary from 100 to 120 days. Sunflower is well adapted to a wide range of climatic and soil conditions. The plant has a finely branched and extensive root system, and for this reason it is an excellent consumer of plant nutrient reserves in the soil. CLIMATE Sunflower is best suited to a semi-arid climate. Normal growth and development occur at temperatures below 35 °C. A minimum annual summer rainfall of 400 mm is required for successful cultivation. Sunflower is not sensitive to day length for flowering. SOIL Sunflower can be grown on sandy loam to heavy clay soils. However, soils prone to surface sealing and eventual crusting can impair plant emergence. During December and January, sand soil temperatures can become so high that they are detrimental to the vigour and emergence of plants. Soils that are brackish or tend to become waterlogged, must also be avoided. Sunflower has a very strong and well-branched taproot system and can achieve lateral and vertical root development of over 2 m. This allows the plant to extract moisture and nutrients from greater depths than most other summer crops. However, any factors inhibiting root development will to a large extent restrict the potential that sunflower has to utilise moisture and nutrients. These factors include nematodes, high levels of exchangeable acidity, soil compaction and excessively wet soils. Sunflower should preferably be preceded by monocotyledon (seeds contain one embryonic leaf) crops such as maize, wheat or sorghum. This will also limit the risk of diseases such as Sclerotinia , which occur in dicotyledon (flowering plants with two embryonic leaves) crops. Effective management of all the nutrient thresholds applicable to these crops is therefore vital in crop rotation systems. YIELD POTENTIAL Estimates for average long term sunflower yields appear in Table 5.5.6.1. The figures take into account rainfall, effective soil depth and clay content. They can be used as a guideline for planning purposes where no prior yield statistics are available. Table 5.5.6.1 Guideline for yield potential of sunflower (in ton ha -1 ) Sunflower is frequently planted too late, with the result that full potential is not achieved. Other factors which often limit yields include a low and uneven stand, ineffective weed control, injudicious fertilization, poorly-adjusted cultivars and the absence of crop rotation, with resultant disease and pest infestation. NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS Although sunflower is a liberal consumer of nutrients, it does not react as favourably to fertilization as does maize. This is due to the good nutrient uptake by the effective root system and the fact that the seed mass constitutes only about a sixth to a seventh of its above ground mass. Table 5.5.6.2 reflects the nutrient removal of sunflower. Table 5.5.6.2 Nutrient removal by 1 ton of sunflower SOIL ACIDITY Sunflower is sensitive to aluminium toxicity. If the soil is very acidic, corrective action must be taken through liming and reducing the acid saturation percentage to as close to 0% as possible. Sunflower fares reasonably well on slightly alkaline soil. MACRO-ELEMENTS Nitrogen (N) Nitrogen is essential for successful sunflower cultivation. Growth and development are drastically reduced by N-deficiencies, leaves become pale green and the bottom leaves die off. A general nitrogen fertilizer guideline, based solely on the yield potential, is given in Table 5.5.6.3. Table 5.5.6.3. Guideline for N-fertilization of sunflower according to yield potential * Levels can be adjusted upwards or downwards by up to 26 kg ha -1 in order to take the available N in the soil profile into account. As sunflower has an effective and deep root system, the plant is able to utilise nitrogen in the soil, even when the quantity thereof is unknown. Consequently, fertilizer applied in accordance with yield potential is often inaccurate. As an alternative, the more accurate delta yield monitoring approach can be used, where the sunflower plant determines the contribution of the soil to the nitrogen nutrition level. Delta yield is the difference in grain yield between the optimum economic yield, or sunflower where the yield is not limited by nitrogen levels, and the yield of a control stand where no nitrogen has been applied. It requires that a control stand or control rows (approximately 1.5% of the area in question is sufficient) in a sunflower field receive no nitrogen fertilizer. The yield of the control stand/row area is deducted from the yield achieved in the remaining area where sufficient fertilizer was applied. The difference between the two yields is the delta yield figure. The difference in yield (delta yield) is thoroughly correlated with the required nitrogen fertilizer level. Table 5.5.6.4 reflects the N-fertilizer requirement for different delta yields. Delta yield gives a perspective on what the optimum N-level would be for a specific season. In order to improve the accuracy of information, it is important to determine a history of nitrogen levels in a region, as well as details of the relevant agricultural practices. Table 5.5.6.4. Guidelines for N-fertilization of sunflower using the delta yield approach Phosphorus (P) Phosphorus is of vital importance for normal development of the sunflower plant. Sunflower reacts well to P-fertilization on soils with a low P-content. Reaction to the application of P is unlikely on soils with good P-reserves. An optimum soil-P level is approximately 14 mg kg -1 P (Bray 1). A phosphorus fertilizer programme should be based on soil analyses. Table 5.5.6.5. Guideline for P-fertilization of sunflower Potassium (K) Although large amounts of potassium are taken up by the sunflower plant, only a relatively small amount is eventually removed by the seed. The reaction to applied-K is very variable and mostly insignificant, and it has not been possible to establish a reliable guideline. This lack of response can be attributed to the fact that sunflower has an efficient root system and is often grown on soils containing sufficient potassium. The availability of potassium can also be influenced by several other factors, such as pH, type of clay mineral, moisture status and K:Mg ratio. Sunflower is a very effective user of K and can take it up more easily than most other crops. See Table 5.5.6.5 for a general potassium fertilizer guideline. Table 5.5.6.6. Guideline for K-fertilization of sunflower* in kg -1 * These are tentative guidelines. Time and method of application The nutrient requirements of the sunflower plant increase greatly after the ten-leaf stage. However, shortages of nutrients in the early growth stages are detrimental and cannot always be rectified by later corrective applications. The total P and K-requirements must therefore be applied before or with planting. Germination can be reduced by excessive N and P, and no more than 25 to 30 kg ha -1 N should be band-placed near the seed. The balance can be placed further away from the seed, or can be applied as a side-dressing. MICRO-ELEMENTS Molybdenum (Mo) Deficiencies occur as early as the seedling stage and are mostly confined to acid soils. Young leaves have a pale appearance and necrosis develops along the edges. Older leaves initially have a pale appearance and as the deficiency increases, leaves become crested and necrotic along the edges. Treating seed with sodium molybdate is effective in preventing deficiencies. As seed supplied by seed companies has generally already been treated with sodium molybdate, the development of a deficiency is unlikely. If deficiency symptoms do occur in seedlings, they can be treated successfully with a foliar spray of 50 g sodium molybdate per 100 l water. Molybdenum deficiencies can almost always be rectified by adequate liming. Boron (B) Sunflower undoubtedly has the highest boron requirement of all agricultural crops cultivated in South Africa. Deficiencies usually occur on leached soils and can seriously suppress crop yields. Deficiencies frequently develop during periods of drought or in instances where the crop is irrigated regularly. Boron deficiencies cause the top leaves to become pale green and to remain small. These leaves often have a soft leather-like texture. Heads can become malformed and are partially filled with dull-coloured seeds. They tend to break off easily at the neck. Flowering is the most sensitive stage for boron deficiency. An application of 1, 2 and 3 kg B ha -1 is recommended for sandy, loam and heavy soils, respectively. Boron should only be applied if soil analyses identify deficiency symptoms and/or if deficiencies have been established by leaf analyses. Excessive boron can suppress yield or damage the follow-up crop. Boron-containing fertilizer compounds or pure boron products such as borax (11.3% B), boric acid (17.5% B) or sodium octaborate (20.5% B) can all be used successfully, provided the levels indicated in the previous paragraph are not exceeded. REFERENCES ASA, CSSA, SSSA., 1997. Sunflower technology and production. International Fertilizer Association, 1992. World Fertilizer use manual. LNR-Instituut vir Graangewasse 1995.
